recurringPattern: Centered error page with a large error code (404/500), a whimsical or abstract illustration of something broken, a short apologetic message ('Something went wrong'), and a 'Go back home' or 'Try again' button.
designHarm: The error page as decoration-first pattern delays the user from solving their actual problem. Large error codes are for developers, not users — they don't help someone recover. The apologetic tone with whimsical illustration creates a mismatch for serious errors (500, payment failure), undermining trust.
legitimateExceptions: Consumer web apps where a lighthearted error tone matches the brand personality
404 pages on content sites where the primary goal is keeping users on-site
possibleFixtures: Standard 404 page with illustration and error code
Recovery-oriented error page with specific user guidance and contact options
Error inline in context instead of full-page takeover
originalObservations: AI-generated error pages consistently display the HTTP status code in large type (72px+) as the primary visual element.
The illustration is always abstract broken-object or disconnected-plug themed.
The 'Go back home' CTA is universal, rarely offering context-specific recovery options.
